Second Party Opinion 
 
Issuer: Department of Airports of the City of Los Angeles 
Issue Description: Los Angeles International Airport Subordinate Refunding Revenue Bonds, 2023 
Series A (Private Activity/AMT) (Green Bonds) 
Project: Airport Improvement Projects 
Green Standard: ICMA Green Bond Principles 
Green Category: Green Buildings 
Keywords: Green buildings, LEED Gold, energy efficiency, airport, gate electrification, net zero 
aligned, Los Angeles, California 
Par: $251,875,000* 
Evaluation Date: February 4, 2023 
*Preliminary, subject to change 
 
GREEN  BONDS DESIGNATION  
Kestrel Verifiers, an Approved Verifier accredited by the Climate Bonds Initiative, conducted an independent 
external review of the Los Angeles International Airport Subordinate Refunding Revenue Bonds, 
2023 Series A (Private Activity/AMT) (Green Bonds) (the “Series 2023A Subordinate Bonds”) to evaluate 
conformance with the Green Bond Principles (June  2021) established by the International Capital Market 
Association. Our team for this engagement included analysts with backgrounds in environmental science. 
This Second Party Opinion reflects our review of the uses and allocation of proceeds , oversight, and 
conformance of the Series 2023A Subordinate Bonds  with the Green Bond Principles. In our opinion, th e 
Series 2023A Subordinate Bonds are impactful, net zero aligned, conform with the four core components 
of the Green Bond Principles, and qualify for Green Bonds designation. 
ABOUT THE ISSUER  
The Department of Airports of the City of Los Angeles (“Department”) is a self -supporting department of 
the City of Los Angeles (the “City”), managing two airports: Los Angeles International Airport (“LAX”) and 
Van Nuys Airport. Both airports contribute significantly to the regional economy, collectively providing more 
than 4,000 jobs.  
The Department has robust environmental commitments which are addressed through energy 
management, reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, water and waste management, noise management, 
land use planning, and regulatory compliance. The 2019 Sustainability Action Plan
1 highlights the 
 
1 “Boldly Moving to Zero: Sustainability Action Plan,” Los Angeles World Airports, 2019, https://www.lawa.org/lawa-sustainability. Kestrel | Second Party Opinion  2 
Department’s history as a leader in airport sustainability and outlines ambitious plans to reach zero carbon 
emissions by 2045 and reduce environmental impacts. The Department has updated the Sustainability 
Action Plan  to align with the Los Angeles Sustainable City pLAn . Major capital projects have been 
strategically selected and designed to minimize energy use and reduce greenhouse gas emissions. In 
2019, Van Nuys Airport built the first of its solar projects and also became the first general aviation airport 
to offer sustainable alternative jet fuel. As of 2021, nearly 30,000 solar panels have been installed and over 
900,000 gallons of sustainable aviation fuel have been delivered.2  
The Department is committed to “boldly moving to zero,” meaning that both LAX and Van Nuys Airport will 
achieve net zero in the following areas by the year 2045: no potable water consumed for non-potable uses, 
zero carbon emissions from the Department’s  operations and zero waste. The Department is also 
committed to using 100% renewable electricity by 2045. Other notable programs and policies include: 
• Sustainable Design and Construction Requirements  – All new buildings and projects at the 
airports must be built to at least LEED Silver standards or the City of Los Angeles Green Building 
Code. 
• Gate Electrification – Gates at LAX have infrastructure which enables planes to use electric power 
while parked, decreasing use of jet fuel and associated emissions.  
• Ground Service Equipment Emission Reduction Policy – Ground Service Equipment Operators are 
required to meet emission reduction goals by 2031.3 
• Alternative Fuel Program at Los Angeles International Airport – All airport lessees and operators 
are required to drive vehicles that are not powered by petroleum-derived gasoline or diesel fuel. As 
of 2019, 100% of airport operators drive compliant vehicles. 
• Electric Vehicle Purchasing Policy – By 2035, 100% of vehicles purchased by the Department will 
be electric, in accordance with the Department’s Electric Vehicle Purchasing Policy.  
• Single-use Plastic Water Bottle Phase-out Policy  – The Department will not allow sale or 
distribution of single-use plastic water bottles at airports after June 2023.  
• Food Donation Program at Los Angeles International Airport – All businesses that provide or sell 
food are required to participate in a food donation program. As of 2021, nearly 15,000 pounds of 
food had been donated. 
• Los Angeles International Airport  Dunes Restoration – The Department dedicated 307 acres on 
the western portion of the airport as a natural wildlife preserve in 1986. Since then, the Department 
has planted native species, eliminated invasive plants, and removed approximately 32,000 square 
feet of hardscape including old streets and sidewalks.  
• El Segundo Blue Butterfly Habitat Management – In 1976, the US Department of Fish and Wildlife 
placed the El Segundo Blue Butterfly on the endangered species list. This butterfly was later found 
in the Los Angeles International Airport  Sand Dunes Habitat. As of 2020, the population of the 
endangered butterfly within the habitat restoration area had grown to 5,300-6,500.  
 
2 “2021 Sustainability Report,” Los  Angeles World Airports, 2021, https://cloud1lawa.app.box.com/s/
pq459i5b4qt4rtw1r2i3296tkixaigxj.   
3 The policy target is to reduce average composite Ground Support Equipment emission factors to equal to, or less than, 1.0 grams 
per horsepower-hour of hydrocarbons plus nitrogen oxides by 2030. Kestrel | Second Party Opinion  3 
The Department was  recognized as a “Business Leader in Air Quality” by the South Coast Air Quality 
Management District and awarded the 2018 Clean Air Award. The Department also received the 2018 
Green Fleet Award from the Green Fleet Awards Forum. In 2021, the Los Angeles A irport Central Utility 
Plant received the Sustainability Leadership Award for Demand Response from Los Angeles Department 
of Water and Power for reducing electricity demand during peak hours.  
ALIGNMENT TO GREEN STANDARDS
4 
Use of Proceeds  
The Series 2023A Subordinate Bonds will refund bonds and notes that finance 
construction and renovation projects (collectively, “Airport Improvement 
Projects”) that meet robust green building standards and advance long- term 
sustainability goals . The Airport Improvement Projects are eligible projects as 
defined by the Green Bond Principles in the Green Buildings project category.   
TBIT West Gates (formerly Midfield Satellite Concourse North):  The Series 2023A  Subordinate Bonds 
refund Subordinate Commercial Paper Notes that financed the Tom Bradley International Terminal (“TBIT”) 
West Gates project, previously named the Midfield Satellite Concourse North Project (“ TBIT West Gates  
Project”).5  
The TBIT West Gates  Project opened in May 2021 and  consists of a new LEED Gold certified multi-level 
15-gate concourse with a cool roof to minimize heat absorption. All gates are equipped with ground power 
and pre-conditioned air to reduce emissions while airplanes are parked . The building includes low-flow 
water fixtures and efficient HVAC and lighting systems. The sustainability features reduce building energy 
use by 29% relative to LEED criteria benchmarks.6 More than 75% of construction waste was recycled. The 
facility is plumbed to use recycled water for restrooms and non-potable uses. Once available, the recycled 
water will be delivered directly from the Hyperion Water Reclamation Plant, operated by the City of Los 
Angeles.  
Bradley West and Terminal 4 Connector:  The Series 2023A Subordinate Bonds refund t he Los Angeles 
International Airport, Senior Revenue Bonds, 2013 Series A (“Series 2013A Bonds”) which financed the  
Bradley West Interior Enhancements Project (“Bradley West”).  
The Bradley West project consisted of improvements to the Tom Bradley Internat ional Terminal and 
construction of a new Terminal 4 Connector Building to join Tom Bradley International Terminal with 
Terminal 4. The overall project resulted in energy -efficient buildings: the Bradley West project achieved 
LEED Gold certification and was  completed in 2015 and the Terminal 4 Connector exceeded the Green 
 
4 Green Bonds are any type of bond instrument where the proceeds will be exclusively applied to finance or refinance eligible Green 
Projects which are aligned with the four core components of ICMA Green Bond Principles.

construction?return=/credits/New%20Construction/v4.1. 
Kestrel | Second Party Opinion  4 
Building Code Tier 2 requirements set forth by the City of Los Angeles. The energy performance of the 
Terminal 4 Connector project exceeds State Green Building code requirements by 37%. 
The Terminal 4 Connector project, completed in 2016, includes space for passenger circulation, baggage 
inspection and handling, security areas, and an outdoor public plaza. Energy- efficient motors in the 
baggage handling system reduced energy consumption by 40%.

Series 2013A Bonds 
While aviation is a significant source of global greenhouse gas emissions, Kestrel views construction of 
airport facilities that meet green building standards as eligible for financing with Green Bond proceeds. In 
Kestrel’s opinion, the mechanisms for reducing the carbon footprint of airline travel may be viewed 
distinctly from the emissions associated with constructing and operating the supporting facilities. 
Net Zero Alignment 
Bonds are net zero aligned if bond -financed activities advance goals to reach net zero greenhouse gas 
emissions by 2050. The Airport Improvement Projects have achieved robust green building certifications 
and incorporate many features to reduce energy demands and associated greenhouse gas emissions. This 
is aligned with the transition to a low -carbon economy. Terminal projects also include gate electrification 
to reduce aircraft emissions while parked. Bond-financed projects directly advance the Department’s goal 
to reach zero carbon emissions by 2045.  
Process for Project Evaluation and Selection  
The Airport Improvement Projects were identified in the 2004 LAX Master Plan which provides a strategic 
framework for expansion and development at the Airport. Priorities identified in the Master Plan were 
subsequently incorporated into the LAX Specific Plan and the Capital Improvement Program. The 
Department’s 2019 Sustainability Action Plan and the City of Los Angeles Sustainable City pLAn (2019)  
guide specific design elements related to energy performance and sustainability in each financed project.  
The Airport Board of Commissioners reviews and approves capital projects ensuring that projects selected 
for financing are aligned with the goals of the Department. 
Management of Proceeds  
Proceeds from the Series 2023A Subordinate Bonds will refund the Series 2013A Bonds, refund a portion 
of the outstanding subordinate commercial paper notes, and pay costs of issuance. Proceeds for the 
refunding will be deposited into the Series 2013A Senior Escrow Fund, overseen by the senior trustee. Prior Kestrel | Second Party Opinion  5 
to allocation, proceeds may be invested in non-callable direct obligations of the United States Treasury or 
held uninvested in cash until May 2023.   
Reporting  
The Department provides comprehensive reporting on progress toward sustainability goals in annual 
Sustainability Reports available at lawa.org/lawa-sustainability. Reported metrics include greenhouse gas 
emissions (MTCO2e), tons of materials recycled, square footage of construction built to green 
building standards, fuel usage and energy usage. In addition, specifically for the Series 2023A 
Subordinate Bonds, the Department expects to provide updates on the allocation of proceeds and 
project-level metrics for energy savings and water savings relative to a baseline, number of gates 
electrified, and square footage of energy-efficient buildings. 
The Department will submit continuing financial disclosures to the Municipal Securities Rulemaking 
Board (“MSRB”) as long as the Series 2023A Subordinate Bonds are outstanding, as well as reports in the 
event of material developments. This reporting will be done annually on the Electronic Municipal Market 
Access (“EMMA”) system operated by the MSRB. 
ALIGNMENT WITH UN SDG s 
 
The Airport Improvement Projects support and advance the vision of the United Nations 
Sustainable Development Goals (“UN SDGs”), including affordable and clean energy: 
 
Affordable and Clean Energy (Target 7.3) 
Incorporate energy-efficient facilities designed to meet LEED Gold certification 
  
Full text of the Target for Goal 7 is available in Appendix A, with additional information available on the 
United Nations website:  un.org/sustainabledevelopment  
CONCLUSION  
Based on our independent  external review, the Los Angeles International Airport Subordinate Refunding 
Revenue Bonds, 2023 Series A (Private Activity/AMT) (Green Bonds) are impactful, net zero aligned , 
conform, in all material respects, with the Green Bond Principles (2021) and are in complete alignment with 
the Green Buildings eligible project category.  The Airport Improvement Projects advance climate action 
goals shared by the Department and City of Los Angeles.

About 
Kestrel provides ESG Impact Data and verification services designed to bring greater transparency and insight to fixed income , 
helping to set the market standard for sustainable finance.  
We are a team of environmental and social scientists, engineers, and finance professionals with deep, nuanced understandings of 
how state and local governments finance and deliver public projects. We understand the  complex activities and infrastructure 
financed with municipal bonds and provide meaningful, material insights on their ESG characteristics with our innovative data  
offering. 
We are also a leading provider of external reviews for green, social and sustainability bond transactions in US public finance, 
consistently garnering over 60% of the market share by par and by number of reviews. We are qualified to evaluate corporate and 
municipal bonds in all asset classes worldwide for conformance with international green and social bond standards.
